does: BlueFin introduces a benchmark dataset of 131 complex, real-world financial spreadsheet tasks designed to evaluate LLM agent performance on synthesis, manipulation, and comprehension activities in professional finance. The benchmark includes 3,225 granular rubric criteria validated by expert human annotators, enabling nuanced evaluation of tasks that resist simple programmatic verification. The work develops an LM judge agent calibrated to achieve parity with expert human evaluation, allowing reliable assessment of agent performance on multifaceted spreadsheet operations that mirror actual workflows in financial services roles.
connects: This work responds to a notable gap in LLM evaluation infrastructure, building on prior benchmarking efforts in code generation and task automation while addressing the understudied spreadsheet domain despite its massive user base. It extends research on evaluating agent capabilities in domain-specific tasks and draws on recent advances in using LM judges for complex evaluation scenarios where ground truth is ambiguous or difficult to specify programmatically. The work connects to broader efforts in benchmarking embodied agent reasoning and tool use, particularly methods that require agents to plan, execute, and verify operations across multiple steps in professional workflows.
enables: BlueFin establishes a foundation for developing specialized LLM agents for financial spreadsheet work, a domain with immediate practical value given the prevalence of spreadsheets in professional settings. The benchmark enables research into better agent architectures for handling complex, multi-step spreadsheet operations and supports evaluation of models on tasks requiring domain-specific financial knowledge combined with tool manipulation skills. Future work can build improved evaluation methods for subjective tasks in specialized domains, develop agents with stronger spreadsheet reasoning capabilities, and extend this framework to other domain-specific agent benchmarking scenarios where expert validation is critical and programmatic evaluation alone is insufficient.
